sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management
b: Birds Bird damage to stone fruit and figs can be severe — flocks of mockingbirds and curve-billed thrashers know when peaches ripen and return daily. The defenses: Netting — physical bird netting (3/4" mesh) draped over the tree from veraison (first color change) to harvest. Most effective; tedious to install on large trees. Harvest just before peak — pick fruit when it's still firm enough to ripen indoors. The window is narrow but reduces in-tree damage. Reflective tape, fake owls — minor effect; birds habituate within 1–2 weeks. Closed-eye fig varieties (Celeste) — bird-resistant compared to

sh: Harvest, storage, and use
b: Fig harvest window · pomegranate storage · drying stone fruit · cider and preservation The orchard year culminates in 4–5 months of harvest, from breba figs in June through Pink Lady apples in November. The Dragoons climate is unusually friendly to the post-harvest stage: low humidity makes drying fruit trivial, dry afternoon air carries away moisture from spread cuttings and racks, and the cool nights stretch the storage life of fall apples and pomegranates.

Pick before hard freeze. | Jujube | Yellow-green with brown speckling (fresh) or brown wrinkled (dried) | Fresh 1–2 weeks; dried 1+ year | Wide window; can dry on tree | Peach | Background color shifts yellow; fragrant; slight give | 5–10 days fresh; cans/freezes well | Picked firm-ripe stores best | Apricot | Full or

sh: Harvest, storage, and use — 2 Drying — the Dragoons advantage
b: Cochise County's low summer humidity (15–30 % outside monsoon) makes drying fruit easier here than anywhere east of California. A simple drying setup: Spread sliced fruit on stainless or food-safe nylon screens Stack screens on the covered porch in afternoon shade Air-dry 3–7 days depending on fruit and weather Finish in a dehydrator at 135 °F for 4–8 hours to ensure shelf-stable moisture content (< 15 %) What dries well: Fruit | Drying notes | Figs | Halve and air-dry. The Mediterranean classic. Mission and Brown Turkey both excellent. | Apricots | Halve, pit, dry skin-down. Bright orange whe

sh: Harvest, storage, and use — 3 Cider, juice, and preservation
b: A small backyard orchard easily produces more apples and pears than a household can eat fresh. The standard preservation directions: Apple cider — press in late October–November (when Granny Smith and Pink Lady are heavy). A small basket press handles a 10–20 tree backyard. Cider freezes well (gallon containers, 4" of headspace); fermented to hard cider with cider yeast; canned as sweet cider with a hot-fill at 185 °F. Pear preserves — Asian pears are best preserved as halves in light syrup; European pears as butter or as the classic pear-honey conserve. Stone fruit canning — peaches and apric

The Holistic Orchard by Michael Phillips — comprehensive low-input orchard management; the dormant-copper and trunk-paint protocols. The Apple Grower by Michael Phillips — the deep apple reference; codling moth IPM and pruning logic. Backyard Fruits and Berries by Diane Relf et al. — the household-scale planning framework. Stark Bro's Nursery (starkbros.com) — bareroot variety descriptions with reliable chill-hour data; the standard mail-order source for Cochise County. sh: Closing
b: A backyard orchard is a multi-year promise. The first fig planted on a January weekend is just a stick in the ground for the first eight weeks, leafs out hesitantly in April, sends up its first slender shoots in May, and produces maybe two figs the first August. The second August, it produces twenty. The third August, eighty. By the fifth August, it is producing 200 figs in a wave that lasts three weeks, and the household has more figs than it knows what to do with, and the freezer is filling with fig jam and the dehydrator is running every afternoon and the wasps and birds are competing with

sh: How to use this guide
b: This guide is built as a reference. Almost no one reads a pest manual cover-to-cover. The way to use it is to know what's in it, scan the table of contents when something shows up in the bed, and read the entry for that one problem. The structure is in three parts: 1. The framework (chapters 1–3). What IPM means, what makes the Dragoons different from other gardens, how to scout, when to act, when to leave it alone. 2. The pest catalog (chapters 4–11). Insects sorted by feeding type (sucking, chewing, soil-dwelling, mites), then diseases by pathogen type (fungal, bacterial, viral), then the ph

sh: The IPM mindset
b: Prevention · scouting · biological controls · targeted intervention Integrated Pest Management (IPM) is the working philosophy this guide is built around. The short version: the spray bottle is the last tool, not the first. Most pest problems in the Dragoons are solved before they happen — by site choice, variety choice, planting date, row cover, sanitation, irrigation discipline, and habitat for beneficial insects. When all of those fail, then you intervene, and you intervene precisely. The four-step IPM logic: 1. Prevention. Right crop, right variety, right timing, right place. A Frost peach

sh: The IPM mindset — 1 The "tolerable damage" mindset
b: The biggest single mental shift for new growers is accepting that a healthy garden has pests in it. A cabbage with three aphids on the outer leaf is not a problem. A cucumber vine with five flea-beetle pinholes per leaf is not a problem. A peach tree with two scab spots out of a hundred fruit is not a problem. The goal is not zero pests; the goal is a stable community where predator and prey hold each other in rough balance and the crop yields enough for the kitchen. Reaching for the spray bottle at the first aphid does three things that work against you over time: Kills the beneficial insects

sh: The IPM mindset — 2 What this guide is, and isn't
b: This is a reference for the home grower and the small market gardener working in the Dragoon Mountains region of Cochise County, Arizona — USDA zone 8a, about 4,700 ft elevation. It is not a commercial pesticide application manual; for licensed applicator material, the University of Arizona Cooperative Extension (Cochise County office) and the Arizona Department of Agriculture are the canonical references. It is also not a veterinary or wildlife-removal manual; for wildlife conflicts beyond exclusion (a sick coyote in the yard, a rattlesnake in the shed), contact Arizona Game and Fish. The con

sh: The Dragoons pest & wildlife landscape
b: What makes Cochise County unique A Pacific-Northwest gardening book and a New-England gardening book are both wrong about the Dragoons in slightly different ways. The pest community here is shaped by four conditions you don't find together anywhere east of the Mississippi: 1. Mild winters. Coldest winter nights are typically 18–25 °F, with hard freezes lasting only 3–6 nights per year. This is not enough to kill the overwintering eggs and adults of most insect pests. Harlequin bug, leafhopper, aphid, and tomato hornworm pupae all overwinter successfully in the Dragoons, which means populations

sh: The Dragoons pest & wildlife landscape — 1 The Dragoons pest "fingerprint"
b: If you've gardened elsewhere, here's what's different. Conditions you're used to | What the Dragoons do instead | Winter kills overwintering pests | Mild winter — most pests overwinter successfully | Slugs are the dominant ground pest | Slugs only matter for 6 weeks post-monsoon | Squash bug is the worst cucurbit pest | Cucumber beetle is worse — bacterial wilt vector | Cabbage looper is the worst brassica pest | Harlequin bug is worse — not affected by Bt | Deer are the worst mammal pressure | Javelina match or exceed deer (and bypass low fences) | Mice and voles are the worst rodent pressure

sh: The Dragoons pest & wildlife landscape — 2 The "year-round" effect
b: In most American climates, January is a reset. Pest populations crash, gardeners regroup. In the Dragoons, January is a slightly slower version of October. Aphids are still on the overwintered kale. Harlequin bug adults are still under the mulch. Packrats are still in the woodpile. The implication: pest management is a 12-month routine, not a March-to-October push. Sanitation in the fall, dormant sprays in January, scouting in February — all of these matter more here than they do in colder country. The single most useful pest-management habit in the Dragoons is the dusk walk. Most pest insects

sh: IPM principles in practice
b: Scouting frequency · action thresholds · biological controls · "tolerable damage" This chapter is the operating manual for the framework in chapter 1. How often to scout, what to look for, what triggers action.

sh: IPM principles in practice — 1 Scouting frequency
b: Crop / context | Scouting frequency | What to look for | Brassica transplants (Aug–Oct) | Every 2 days | Cabbage white eggs (yellow, on leaf underside); harlequin bug adults; slug damage | Cucurbits (Jul–Sep) | Every 2 days | Cucumber beetle adults; squash bug egg clusters (copper-colored, on leaf underside); wilt symptoms | Tomato (Jul–Sep) | Every 3 days | Hornworm frass on leaves below feeding sites; psyllid yellow-stippling on new growth | Peppers (May–Oct) | Weekly | Aphid clusters on growing tips; thrip silvery scarring on leaves | Grape (May–Sep) | Weekly + storm-trigger | Powdery milde

sh: IPM principles in practice — 2 Action thresholds — when to intervene
b: The honest answer is: it depends on the crop and the pest. Some workable defaults: Pest | Action threshold | Reasoning | Aphids on growing tips | Visible curl of new growth, or 30+ per tip | Below this, predators usually catch up | Spider mites | Visible stippling on >10 % of leaves | Mites explode quickly past this point | Cucumber beetle | Any beetle, on any cucurbit | Vector for bacterial wilt — one bite can transmit | Squash vine borer eggs | Any egg, on any squash | Single bore = dead vine; prevention only | Cabbage white caterpillars | 1+ caterpillar per plant | Multiplies fast; Bt at fi

sh: IPM principles in practice — 3 Biological controls — the food web
b: The Dragoons have an unusually rich beneficial-insect community when you stop spraying for it. The main categories: Parasitic wasps (Cotesia, Trichogramma, Aphidius) — tiny, mostly invisible, lay eggs in caterpillars and aphids. The mummified-aphid skins on a tomato plant are evidence of Aphidius. The grain-of-rice cocoons on a hornworm's back are Cotesia. Both attracted by small flowering plants (cilantro, dill, fennel, yarrow, sweet alyssum). Lacewings (Chrysoperla) — green adult; larvae are voracious aphid and mite predators. Ladybugs (Hippodamia convergens native; Harmonia axyridis invasiv

sh: IPM principles in practice — 4 Hydroponic vs soil — different pest profiles
b: The two production systems have different pest communities. Knowing the difference saves a lot of misdiagnosis. Pest / problem | Soil bed | Hydroponic | Cutworms | Yes — at transplant | No — irrelevant indoors / no soil | Grasshoppers | Yes (drought years) | Only outdoor Bato buckets | Slugs | Yes — post-monsoon | Rare; sometimes on rockwool | Aphids, thrips, mites | Yes | Yes — same control | Cucumber beetle | Yes | Yes if outdoor; rare indoors | Squash vine borer | Yes | Rare — no soil for pupation | Soil-borne fungal disease (Fusarium, Verticillium) | Yes | No — system can be flushed | Root

sh: Sucking insects
b: Aphids · thrips · whitefly · leafhoppers · psyllid · mealybug · scale Sucking insects insert a piercing-sucking mouthpart into plant tissue and feed on sap. Damage shows as stippling, distortion, sticky honeydew (and the sooty mold that grows on it), curled leaves, and stunting. Several species also vector viruses. As a group, sucking insects respond to: water sprays (mechanical knockdown), insecticidal soap, neem oil, and the predators (lacewings, ladybugs, syrphid flies, parasitic wasps) that handle them invisibly when not sprayed.

sh: Sucking insects — 1 Aphids
b: There are roughly 5,000 aphid species globally; the Dragoons see about a dozen with any regularity. The four you'll meet: Species | Color | Main hosts | Notes | Green peach aphid (Myzus persicae) | Pale green | Peppers, peaches, brassicas, many | Vectors >100 plant viruses | Cabbage aphid (Brevicoryne brassicae) | Gray-green, waxy | Brassicas only | Heavy waxy coat resists soap | Cotton/melon aphid (Aphis gossypii) | Yellow-green | Cucurbits | Vectors cucumber mosaic | Woolly apple aphid (Eriosoma lanigerum) | White fuzz on bark | Apple roots, branches | Hardest to control; serious in pomes |

sh: Sucking insects — 2 Thrips
b: Thrips are tiny (1–2 mm) slender insects that rasp leaf surfaces and suck up the exuding sap. The two species that matter: Onion thrips (Thrips tabaci) — alliums primarily, also brassicas; silvery flecks on inner leaf curl. Western flower thrips (Frankliniella occidentalis) — peppers, tomatoes, flowers; vectors tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV). Identification. Silvery, papery scars on leaves and fruit. Tiny black "frass dots" along with the silvering. The insects themselves are hard to see; tap a flower over a white sheet and watch for cream-colored slivers running across. Scouting. Yellow or

sh: Sucking insects — 3 Whitefly
b: Tiny (1.5 mm) white moth-like insects that fly up in a cloud when a leaf is disturbed. The two species: Greenhouse whitefly (Trialeurodes vaporariorum) — common in indoor and protected production. Silverleaf / sweetpotato whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) — outdoor in warm regions; vectors several geminiviruses. Identification. White cloud rising from leaf undersides when disturbed. Yellow stippling on upper leaf. Honeydew and sooty mold below colony. Control sequence. 1. Yellow sticky traps at canopy height — catches adults; primarily for monitoring. 2. Insecticidal soap weekly until population break

sh: Sucking insects — 4 Leafhoppers and sharpshooters
b: Wedge-shaped jumping insects, 3–5 mm, that run sideways on stems and jump away when disturbed. They feed on plant sap and several species vector serious diseases. Beet leafhopper (Circulifer tenellus) — vectors curly top virus on tomatoes, beans, peppers, beets. Glassy-winged sharpshooter (Homalodisca vitripennis) — vectors Pierce's disease on grapes. Cold-limited; rare at Dragoons elevation (4,500+ ft) but present at lower Cochise sites. Potato leafhopper (Empoasca fabae) — causes "hopperburn" stippling and necrotic margins on beans, potatoes. Identification. White or yellow stippling on leav

sh: Sucking insects — 5 Psyllid
b: Looks like a tiny cicada (2–3 mm). Adults jump or fly; nymphs are flattened scale-like discs that stay put on leaf undersides. Tomato/potato psyllid (Bactericera cockerelli) — vectors zebra chip in potato and "psyllid yellows" in tomato. Major Southwest pest in dry warm springs. Identification. Yellowed cupping of new growth on tomato and potato (the "yellows" symptom). Tiny flat nymphs (like a flat scale insect) on leaf undersides. Sticky honeydew with a granular white "psyllid sugar" on leaves. Control sequence. 1. Row cover at transplant through May. 2. Sulphur dust — disrupts psyllid popul

sh: Sucking insects — 6 Mealybugs and scale
b: Less common in outdoor Dragoons gardens but persistent on citrus, indoor figs, hibiscus, and overwintered hydroponic plants. Mealybugs — cottony white masses in leaf axils. Treat with rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab, or repeated insecticidal-soap sprays. Persistent — don't expect a single application to fix it. Scale insects — bumpy crust on bark or stems, often along the underside of leaf midribs. Soft scale (sticky, exudes honeydew) and armored scale (no honeydew, harder shell). Dormant-season horticultural oil is the standard control. Encourage predatory ladybugs (the Lindorus / Rhyzobius

sh: Sucking insects — 7 Reference table — sucking insects at a glance
b: Pest | Season | Crops | Severity | First-line control | Green peach aphid | Oct–Apr | Peppers, brassicas, peach | Moderate | Hose blast | Cabbage aphid | Nov–Feb | Brassicas | High in late fall | Hose blast + soap | Cotton aphid | Jul–Sep | Cucurbits | Moderate | Predators, soap | Woolly apple aphid | All season | Apple | Moderate | Dormant oil | Onion thrips | Mar–May | Alliums | Low | Hose blast | Western flower thrips | Apr–Sep | Peppers, tomato, flowers | Moderate | Spinosad | Whitefly | Indoor / warm season | Indoor crops, tomato | Moderate | Soap + sticky traps | Beet leafhopper | Apr–Ju

sh: Chewing insects
b: Caterpillars · beetles · borers · grasshoppers Chewing insects remove plant tissue with mandibles. Damage shows as missing tissue (holes, notches, defoliation), frass (insect droppings), and sometimes stem severing or fruit boring. Many can be hand-picked, which is the highest-return single tool for this group on small plots. Where spraying is needed, Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) — a soil bacterium toxic to caterpillars but harmless to bees, beneficials, mammals, and birds — is the canonical first reach for caterpillar pests. Bt does not affect beetles or true bugs.

sh: Chewing insects — 1 Cabbage white & cabbage looper
b: The two most common brassica caterpillars in the Dragoons. Cabbage white butterfly (Pieris rapae) — small white butterfly with one to two black spots per wing. Lays single yellow eggs (1 mm, bullet-shaped) on the underside of brassica leaves. Larvae are bright green and velvety, growing to about 30 mm. Cabbage looper (Trichoplusia ni) — adult is a nondescript brown moth; larva is similar to the cabbage white green caterpillar but moves in a looping "inchworm" posture and is more nocturnal. Crops affected. All brassicas (cabbage, broccoli, cauliflower, kale, brussels sprouts, kohlrabi, collards

sh: Chewing insects — 2 Hornworms
b: Two related species — the tomato hornworm (Manduca quinquemaculata) and the tobacco hornworm (Manduca sexta) — feed on Solanaceae (tomato, pepper, eggplant, potato). The tobacco hornworm is more common in the Southwest. Both are large (up to 100 mm), green, with a curved spine ("horn") on the rear. Identification. Large green caterpillars on tomato or pepper foliage. Sudden defoliation of a single stem overnight. Pellet-sized green-black frass on lower leaves. The caterpillar itself is famously hard to see — its color matches the leaf exactly — but you can usually spot the frass first, then lo

sh: Chewing insects — 3 Harlequin bug — THE Dragoons brassica pest
b: The signature local brassica pest, and one of the few that's worse in the Dragoons than in most of the country. Murgantia histrionica — bright orange and black shield-shaped true bug, 8–12 mm. Adults overwinter in plant debris and emerge in late summer to attack fall brassicas. Harlequin bug is THE Dragoons brassica pest. It is not affected by Bt. It is hard to kill once established. It is the single biggest reason fall brassica plantings fail in Cochise County. Plan exclusion (row cover + sanitation) into the brassica calendar — before the bug appears, not after. Identification. Vivid orange-

sh: Chewing insects — 4 Cucumber beetle — the cucurbit vector
b: The Dragoons signature cucurbit pest, and the most important warm-season pest in the whole guide because of its disease-vector role. See Guide 7 §5.5 for the full protocol; what follows is the reference summary. Two species both present: Striped cucumber beetle (Acalymma vittatum) — yellow with three black stripes. Spotted cucumber beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) — yellow with twelve black spots. Cucumber beetle = bacterial wilt vector. The beetle by itself eats a small amount of foliage and flowers — annoying but not crop-threatening. The reason it's the signature cucurbit pest is that it

sh: Chewing insects — 5 Squash vine borer
b: A clearwing moth (Melittia cucurbitae) whose larva bores into squash stems and kills the plant from inside. The biggest single-plant killer in the Dragoons cucurbit patch. See Guide 7 §6.4 for the full protocol. Identification. Sudden wilt of an entire squash or zucchini plant despite full moisture. Frass (sawdust-like) at the base of the main stem. Slicing the stem lengthwise reveals a fat cream-colored caterpillar tunneling inside. Life cycle. Single adult emergence in mid-to-late July in the Dragoons. Adult resembles a wasp (orange and black, day-flying). Lays eggs at base of squash stems.

sh: Chewing insects — 6 Corn earworm
b: The single most reliable pest of sweet corn in the Dragoons. Helicoverpa zea — the larva of a brown night-flying moth. Same species attacks tomato (tomato fruitworm), cotton (cotton bollworm), and bean (bean podworm). Identification. Cream to brown caterpillars (up to 35 mm) inside the ear tips, eating kernels. Frass at the silks. The ear tip is the only part affected — kernels below the feeding zone are usually fine. Life cycle. Adult moth lays single eggs on fresh corn silks. Larva crawls down the silk into the ear tip. Three or four generations per year in southern Arizona; peak August. Con

sh: Chewing insects — 7 Codling moth — the apple pest
b: Cydia pomonella. The dominant pest of apple and pear in the Dragoons. See Guide 9 §10 for the full orchard protocol. Identification. Wormy fruit — small caterpillars eating into the fruit, usually entering through the calyx (blossom) end. Frass at the entry hole. Apples drop prematurely. Life cycle. Three generations per year in the long Dragoons apple season — late April, late June, and late August. Adult is a small gray moth with bronze wing-tips; flies at dusk. Control sequence. 1. Pheromone traps for monitoring. Threshold: 5+ moths per trap per week triggers spray. 2. Bag fruit individuall

sh: Chewing insects — 8 Flea beetle
b: Tiny (2 mm) shiny black beetles that jump when disturbed, leaving pinholes in leaves. Phyllotreta and Epitrix species. Multiple species attack different crops. Identification. Pinholes on leaves; tiny black beetles that jump away when leaves are touched. Worst on young seedlings; mature plants outgrow the damage. Crops affected. Eggplant primarily; mustards, arugula, and young brassicas secondary; tomato and sweet potato occasionally. Control sequence. 1. Row cover at transplant through about 4 weeks of growth. 2. Tolerate on established plants — cosmetic damage only. 3. Nasturtiums as trap cr

sh: Chewing insects — 9 Blister beetle
b: Long (15–25 mm) slender beetles, often striped or black, that appear in swarms after monsoon rains. Epicauta species. Eat tomato, potato, pepper, and amaranth foliage. Notable because they contain cantharidin — a chemical that blisters human skin on contact. Identification. Sudden swarm of long beetles on tomato or pepper plants, often defoliating an entire plant in hours. Black, gray, or striped pattern. Control sequence. 1. Hand-pick wearing gloves. Cantharidin causes blisters; do not touch beetles directly. 2. Knock into a bucket of soapy water with a stick. 3. Row cover on susceptible crop

sh: Chewing insects — 10 Grasshoppers
b: In drought years, a serious pest of nearly everything. Three or four species in the Dragoons; Melanoplus species dominant. Identification. Rapid defoliation, no specific pattern. Visible grasshoppers, especially in late afternoon. Control sequence. 1. Row cover during heavy years. 2. Nosema locustae bait — protozoan biocontrol that infects grasshoppers; slow-acting and works only on nymphs. Apply early in season. 3. Kaolin clay spray — coats foliage with a film that grasshoppers won't feed on. Hard to keep on through monsoon rain. 4. Chickens or guinea fowl if you keep them — significant contr

sh: Chewing insects — 11 Reference table — chewing insects
b: Pest | Season | Crops | Severity | First-line control | Cabbage white | Aug–Oct, Mar–May | Brassicas | Moderate | Row cover + Bt | Cabbage looper | Sep–Nov | Brassicas | Moderate | Bt | Harlequin bug | Sep–Nov | Brassicas | High — signature | Sanitation + row cover | Tomato hornworm | Jul–Sep | Solanaceae | High | Dusk hand-pick | Cucumber beetle | Jul–Sep | Cucurbits | High — signature | Exclusion + dusk spray | Squash vine borer | Jul (1 flight) | Squash, zucchini | High | Foil collar | Corn earworm | Aug | Sweet corn | High | Early planting, mineral oil | Codling moth | May–Aug | Apple, pea

sh: Soil pests
b: Wireworm · cutworm · root-knot nematode · root maggot · grubs Soil-dwelling pests are the ones you can't scout for directly — you see the damage above ground and infer what's eating roots below. Most soil pest management is preventive: cover crops, rotation, solarization, and exclusion at transplant.

sh: Soil pests — 1 Cutworm
b: Gray-brown caterpillars (15–40 mm) that curl into a "C" when disturbed. Agrotis and Peridroma species. Hide in soil during day, emerge at night, sever transplant stems at ground level. Identification. Seedlings or transplants cleanly cut off at soil level, lying intact on the ground in the morning. Sometimes the caterpillar is curled in the soil within 2 inches of the damaged plant. Crops affected. Brassica transplants, tomato transplants, lettuce, anything at the small-transplant stage in August–October. Spring transplants in March–April. Life cycle. Multiple species; some overwinter as larva

sh: Soil pests — 2 Wireworm
b: Hard, shiny, slender orange-yellow larvae (15–30 mm) of click beetles (Agriotes, Limonius). Bore into root crops, seed potatoes, and tubers. Identification. Slender brown tunnels in carrots, potatoes, sweet potatoes, and root crops generally. Wireworms themselves are found in soil during cultivation. Crops affected. Root crops primarily; corn and bean seedlings if heavy. Life cycle. Adults are click beetles. Larvae live 2–4 years in soil before pupating. Worse in beds recently converted from sod or pasture (where larvae lived in grass roots). Control sequence. 1. Avoid planting root crops in n

sh: Soil pests — 3 Root-knot nematode
b: Microscopic worms (Meloidogyne species) that infect roots and cause galls, stunting, and progressive decline. Persistent in beds once established. Identification. Stunted above-ground growth despite adequate water and nutrients. Roots show small to large galls (bumpy lumpy swellings) when dug. Worse on carrots, beets, tomatoes, peppers, melons. Crops affected. Carrots and root crops show galls most dramatically; tomatoes, melons, and peppers are also susceptible. Brassicas, alliums, and corn are tolerant. Life cycle. Persistent in soil. One generation per 30–60 days during warm weather. Female

sh: Soil pests — 4 Root maggot
b: Delia species — the larvae of small flies that lay eggs at the soil line on brassica and onion stems. Larvae tunnel into roots and bulbs. Cabbage maggot (Delia radicum) — brassica roots and stem base. Onion maggot (Delia antiqua) — onion and garlic bulbs. Identification. Wilting brassica transplant without obvious above-ground cause; pull up and find white tunneling larvae at the root crown. Onions with brown rotted-out bulb interiors and tunneling. Control sequence. 1. Row cover at transplant prevents adult egg-lay. Single most effective intervention. 2. Wood-ash or diatomaceous earth ring ar

sh: Soil pests — 5 White grubs
b: Larvae of June beetles, May beetles, and chafers. Large (15–30 mm), white, C-curled grubs in soil. Eat roots of grasses, sometimes seedlings. Identification. White C-shaped grubs found during cultivation. Usually a problem only in beds recently converted from lawn or in beds heavily amended with raw organic matter. Control sequence. 1. Beneficial nematodes (Heterorhabditis bacteriophora) — soil drench in warm soil. 2. Milky spore disease — bacterial control, slow to establish but persistent. 3. Cultivation. Tilling exposes grubs to predator birds.

sh: Soil pests — 6 Reference — soil pests
b: Pest | Damage | Crops | Control sequence | Cutworm | Severed transplants overnight | Brassicas, tomato | Cardboard collars | Wireworm | Tunneled roots | Carrots, potatoes | Rotation, traps | Root-knot nematode | Galled roots, stunting | Tomato, carrot, melon | Marigold cover, rotation, resistant varieties | Cabbage maggot | Tunneled brassica roots | Brassicas | Row cover at transplant | Onion maggot | Rotted onion bulbs | Alliums | Row cover at transplant | White grubs | Root feeding | Lawn, seedlings | Beneficial nematodes |

sh: Mites & non-insect pests
b: Spider mites · eriophyid mites · springtails Mites are not insects — they're arachnids, with eight legs as adults (six as larvae). Most are too small to see clearly without a hand lens. They reproduce explosively in dry hot weather and resist many conventional insecticides.

sh: Mites & non-insect pests — 1 Spider mites
b: Two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae) is the dominant species. Adults are about 0.4 mm with two dark spots on the back. They feed on undersides of leaves and produce fine webbing. Crops affected. Almost everything — peppers, tomatoes, eggplant, beans, melons, hops, citrus, almost all hydroponic crops. Severe in dry heat (June pre-monsoon especially). Identification. Tiny stippled white-yellow flecking on leaves (each fleck is a feeding site). Fine webbing on leaf undersides and growing tips in heavy infestations. The mites themselves require a hand lens — look like moving specks of dus

sh: Mites & non-insect pests — 2 Eriophyid mites
b: Microscopic (0.2 mm) mites that cause specific deformations rather than general stippling. Genus-specific: Tomato russet mite (Aculops lycopersici) — causes bronzing of lower tomato stems and leaves; severe in hot dry conditions. Grape erineum mite (Colomerus vitis) — causes raised galls (erineum patches) on grape leaves; cosmetic. Apple rust mite (Aculus schlechtendali) — causes russeting of apple skin; minor. Identification. Specific symptom by host — bronzed tomato stems, raised galls on grape, rusty apple skin. The mites themselves are not visible without strong magnification. Control sequ

sh: Mites & non-insect pests — 3 Springtails
b: Tiny (1–3 mm) jumping arthropods that thrive in moist soil and decomposing organic matter. Not true insects, not true pests in most cases. Sometimes a nuisance in hydroponic rockwool and seedling trays. Control. Reduce moisture; springtails self-correct when conditions dry. No spray needed.

sh: Fungal diseases
b: Powdery mildew · peach leaf curl · early blight · late blight · downy mildew · Fusarium · Verticillium · gray mold · Septoria · Pythium Fungal diseases in the Dragoons are seasonal — the dry air kills most fungi for nine months of the year, but the six-week monsoon window is a concentrated risk. The dominant principle for fungal disease is prevention before symptoms appear. Once you see disease, the season's outcome is mostly fixed; you can slow it but rarely stop it. Two times to act: dormant winter sprays before bud break (for peach leaf curl, brown rot), and preventive sprays in late June b

sh: Fungal diseases — 1 Powdery mildew
b: The most common fungal disease in the Dragoons. Multiple causal species — Podosphaera xanthii on cucurbits, Erysiphe species on others, Uncinula necator on grape. Identification. White powdery coating on upper leaf surfaces, sometimes shoots and stems. Affected leaves yellow and drop. Heavily affected fruit (cucumbers, melons) can develop sunburn from leaf loss. Crops affected. Squash, zucchini, cucumber, melon, grape, peppers (mild), peas, beans (late). Almost everything is susceptible to some species. Life cycle. Spores germinate at 60–80 °F under humid conditions but unlike most fungi do no

sh: Fungal diseases — 2 Downy mildew
b: Different organism (oomycete, Peronospora and Pseudoperonospora species) than powdery mildew but similar appearance. Less common in the Dragoons because it needs continuous leaf wetness — which the dry-day-wet-night monsoon pattern doesn't always provide. Identification. Yellow angular patches on upper leaf, with a downy gray-purple fuzz on the underside (visible early morning when dew is fresh). Worse on cucurbits, brassicas, alliums. Control sequence. Same as powdery mildew, with copper-based sprays added for severe cases. Less common in the Dragoons than in humid climates.

sh: Fungal diseases — 3 Peach leaf curl
b: The single most important stone-fruit disease in the Dragoons. Caused by Taphrina deformans. Infects buds during winter and emerges with the new spring leaves. Two dormant copper sprays, January and February. This is the single most important stone-fruit task in the Dragoon orchard calendar. Skip it and the peach crop is at risk. Do it religiously and peach leaf curl essentially never appears. Once symptoms appear in spring, control for that season is impossible. Identification. Red puckered, thickened, curled leaves on peach or nectarine in early spring (March–April). Severe defoliation. Foll

sh: Fungal diseases — 4 Early blight
b: Caused by Alternaria solani. Most common on tomato; also on potato and eggplant. Identification. Concentric brown ring spots on lower leaves first; spots have a "bullseye" appearance. Yellowing and drop of affected leaves. Progresses up the plant. Life cycle. Overwinters on debris and in soil. Spreads by water splash. Peak during monsoon. Worse on stressed, low-nutrient plants. Control sequence. 1. Mulch heavily to reduce soil splash onto lower leaves. 2. Prune off lowest 12 inches of foliage on tomatoes. 3. Drip irrigation, not overhead. 4. Crop rotation — no tomatoes in the same bed for 3 ye

sh: Fungal diseases — 5 Late blight
b: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Devastating in humid climates; rare in the Dragoons because the dry mid-day breaks the disease cycle. Identification. Olive-green or brown water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems. White fuzzy growth on undersides in humid weather. Fruit develops greasy brown lesions. Rapid death. Control. If late blight appears (rare): remove and destroy all infected plant material; copper or chlorothalonil spray as preventive. Plant in well-spaced sunny beds with airflow.

sh: Fungal diseases — 6 Fusarium and Verticillium wilts
b: Two distinct soilborne fungi (Fusarium oxysporum and Verticillium dahliae) with similar symptoms. Both invade roots and plug vascular tissue. Identification. One-sided yellowing of lower leaves on tomato (Fusarium often), bilateral on eggplant (Verticillium often). Wilting progresses upward despite adequate water. Brown discoloration in stem vascular tissue when split. Life cycle. Persist 6+ years in soil. Spread by infected transplants, contaminated tools. Control sequence. 1. Resistant varieties. "VF" (Verticillium + Fusarium resistant) or "VFN" (with nematode resistance) on tomato seed pack

sh: Fungal diseases — 7 Gray mold (Botrytis)
b: Botrytis cinerea. The "noble rot" of wine grapes and the common cause of post-bloom decline on many fruits. Identification. Fuzzy gray mold on flowers, fruit, leaves. Worst on dense canopy fruit zones. Tomato fruit develops "ghost spot" rings; grape clusters get bunch rot. Crops affected. Strawberry, grape, tomato, peppers, beans (post-bloom), figs. Life cycle. Spores overwinter on debris. Cool wet conditions favor development — paradoxically less of an issue in hot midsummer than in cool monsoon mornings. Control sequence. 1. Airflow. Open canopies, space plants, prune dense foliage. 2. Mulch

sh: Fungal diseases — 8 Septoria leaf spot
b: Septoria lycopersici on tomato primarily. Small dark spots with light centers on lower leaves, progressing upward. Control. Same as early blight — mulch, prune lower foliage, drip irrigation, rotation.

sh: Fungal diseases — 9 Pythium root rot
b: The dominant hydroponic-system pathogen. Pythium species are oomycetes that attack roots in warm reservoirs (above 72 °F). Identification. Brown slimy roots in net pots; sour smell from reservoir; wilting despite full reservoir. Control sequence. See Guide 1 §8 and Guide 2 for full protocol. 1. Drop reservoir temperature below 72 °F. Bury, insulate, shade. 2. Flush system, replace media if roots are brown. 3. Hydrogen peroxide at 3 ml/gal of 3% — dissolves bio-film, oxygenates root zone.

sh: Fungal diseases — 10 Other fungal issues
b: Disease | Pathogen | Crops | Key control | Apple scab | Venturia inaequalis | Apple | Resistant varieties, sanitation; rare in Dragoons | Brown rot | Monilinia spp. | Stone fruit | Copper at bloom; thin for airflow | Fire-blight (bacterial; see §9) | Erwinia amylovora | Apple, pear | Prune 12" below damage | Anthracnose | Colletotrichum spp. | Tomato, pepper, beans | Sanitation, crop rotation | Damping-off | Pythium, Rhizoctonia | Seedlings | Sterile seed-starting medium, airflow |

sh: Bacterial diseases
b: Bacterial wilt · Pierce's disease · fireblight · bacterial speck/spot · soft rot Bacterial diseases are less common than fungal in the Dragoons but harder to control. Most have no chemical cure — the management is prevention (resistant varieties, vector control, sanitation) and removal of infected material.

sh: Bacterial diseases — 1 Bacterial wilt of cucurbits
b: Erwinia tracheiphila. The reason cucumber beetle is the Dragoons signature cucurbit pest. Identification. Sudden wilt of a cucumber, melon, or squash vine despite adequate moisture. Cut a stem and a sticky stream of bacterial ooze pulls between the cut faces when separated. Death within 7–14 days of first symptoms. Crops affected. Cucumber and melon especially; squash and zucchini less commonly. Life cycle. Overwinters in cucumber beetle gut. Transmitted when beetle feeds on a healthy plant. Control. No cure once infected. Management is vector control — see §5.4 for cucumber beetle protocol, a

sh: Bacterial diseases — 2 Pierce's disease
b: Xylella fastidiosa. Bacterial disease of grapes and many other plants, vectored by xylem-feeding leafhoppers (sharpshooters). Identification. Marginal leaf scorch on grape (yellow to brown drying from the leaf edge inward, with a green-yellow border). Progressive vine decline over 2–5 years. Status in the Dragoons. Pierce's disease is essentially absent above 4,500 ft elevation. The bacterium does not survive Dragoons winter cold; vector populations are limited. The threat lies at lower-elevation Cochise sites. See Guide 5 §1 for the elevation-rule logic. Control. Site selection is everything

sh: Bacterial diseases — 3 Fireblight
b: Erwinia amylovora. The dominant bacterial disease of apple and pear in the Dragoons. Moderate pressure here — drier than Pacific Northwest pear country but a real concern in monsoon years. Identification. "Shepherd's crook" — blackened bent tips on branches; shoot tips look scorched. Cankers (dark sunken bark patches) on larger branches. Bacterial ooze in warm wet weather. Crops affected. Apple, pear, and quince. Asian pears are much more resistant than European pears. Life cycle. Bacteria overwinter in cankers; spread by rain and insects during bloom. Worst in warm wet springs. Control sequen

sh: Bacterial diseases — 4 Bacterial speck and spot
b: Pseudomonas syringae (speck) and Xanthomonas species (spot). Cool wet spring weather diseases on tomato and peppers. Identification. Small black spots with yellow halos on leaves and fruit. Fruit spots are small, dark, often scabby. Control. 1. Disease-free seed — these bacteria spread on seed. 2. Drip irrigation, not overhead. 3. Copper spray weekly during cool wet spring. 4. Crop rotation.

sh: Bacterial diseases — 5 Soft rot
b: Erwinia carotovora and related species. Causes wet, foul-smelling rot of stored or wounded vegetables — onions, potatoes, cabbage heads, carrots, sweet peppers. Control. Prevention is everything: handle gently at harvest, cure properly, store dry. Once started, the rot spreads through the storage container.

sh: Viral diseases
b: Tomato/tobacco mosaic · curly top · TSWV · cucumber mosaic Viral diseases have no cure. Management is vector control (the insect that carries the virus), resistant varieties, and removal of infected plants. All of them are common enough in the Dragoons that the home grower will encounter at least one in a typical season.

sh: Viral diseases — 1 Tomato/tobacco mosaic virus (TMV/ToMV)
b: Mosaic-pattern yellowing on leaves; distorted growth. Vectored mechanically (on hands, tools, clothing — anyone who smokes tobacco can transfer ToMV from cigarette to plant). Persistent in soil. Control. 1. Resistant varieties — most modern hybrid tomatoes carry the Tm-1 or Tm-2 resistance gene. 2. Wash hands before handling tomatoes. Especially after smoking. 3. Remove infected plants within 48 hours. Trash, don't compost. 4. Sterilize tools with 10 % bleach between plants when pruning.

sh: Viral diseases — 2 Curly top virus (BCTV)
b: Beet curly top virus. Vectored by the beet leafhopper. Year-dependent severity in the Dragoons — devastating in some years, absent in others. Identification. Stunted, upward-cupped leaves with yellowed veins. Plants stop growing and produce no fruit. Affects tomato, bean, beet, spinach, pepper. Control. 1. Row cover at transplant in known bad years. 2. Resistant varieties — fewer than for TMV, but some exist (Roza tomato is curly-top tolerant). 3. Remove infected plants — they're not coming back. 4. Plant earlier — establish the crop before the May–June peak of leafhopper migration.

sh: Viral diseases — 3 Tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV)
b: Vectored by western flower thrips. Causes a wide range of symptoms — bronzing of leaves, ringspots on fruit, stunting. Control. 1. Resistant varieties. Most quality tomato hybrids now include "SW" (TSWV) resistance. 2. Thrips control — reflective mulch, hose blast, spinosad. 3. Remove infected plants within 48 hours.

sh: Viral diseases — 4 Cucumber mosaic virus (CMV)
b: Vectored by aphids. Wide host range — cucurbits, peppers, tomatoes, ornamentals. Identification. Yellow mosaic pattern on leaves; distorted fruit (cucumber fruit becomes warty and bitter). Stunted growth. Control. 1. Aphid control — see §4.1. 2. Resistant varieties in cucumber (Marketmore 76, Diva). 3. Remove infected plants.

sh: Viral diseases — 5 Reference — viral diseases
b: Virus | Vector | Hosts | Severity | Control | TMV/ToMV | Mechanical | Tomato, pepper, eggplant | Moderate | Resistant varieties, hygiene | Curly top | Beet leafhopper | Tomato, bean, beet | Year-dependent | Row cover, resistant varieties | TSWV | Western flower thrips | Tomato, pepper, lettuce | Moderate | Resistant varieties, thrips control | Cucumber mosaic | Aphids | Cucurbits, peppers | Moderate | Resistant varieties, aphid control |

sh: Physiological disorders
b: Blossom-end rot · hollow stem · tipburn · buttoning · sunscald · splitting Physiological disorders look like disease but aren't caused by pathogens. They're caused by environmental stress, nutrient imbalance, or cultivar limitations. The Dragoons climate produces a particular set of them — wide diurnal swings, intense sun, dry air, alkaline soil, monsoon rain shock — and several are major causes of crop loss that get misdiagnosed as disease.

sh: Physiological disorders — 1 Blossom-end rot (BER)
b: Dark sunken spot on the blossom end of tomato, pepper, eggplant, squash, watermelon. Cause: calcium not reaching the developing fruit — almost always a delivery problem (irregular watering, root damage, pH imbalance) rather than a supply problem (true calcium deficiency in soil or nutrient solution). Soil control. Consistent watering. Mulch to even out soil moisture. Soil pH 6.0–7.0 (alkaline Cochise water is usually fine for calcium availability). Avoid excessive nitrogen, which dilutes calcium uptake. Hydroponic control. See Guide 3 Part 1. Maintain pH 5.8–6.2, EC below 3.0, and mix calcium

sh: Physiological disorders — 2 Tipburn
b: Brown necrotic margins on inner leaves of lettuce, cabbage, brussels sprouts, and other heading crops. Caused by calcium failure during rapid growth, often combined with heat stress. Same root mechanism as BER — calcium is the most water-mobility-dependent macronutrient. Control. Even watering. Avoid heat stress (shade cloth in late spring); choose tipburn-resistant varieties (modern lettuce hybrids commonly are).

sh: Physiological disorders — 3 Hollow stem
b: Hollow cavities in the main stem and floret stems of broccoli and cauliflower. Caused by boron deficiency, often combined with very rapid growth (high nitrogen + plenty of water + warm soil). Control. Soil test for boron. Side-dress with boron only if test shows deficit — boron toxicity is easy to cause. Slow growth slightly with moderate nitrogen.

sh: Physiological disorders — 4 Buttoning
b: Premature, tiny head formation on brassicas — broccoli or cauliflower that produces a marble-sized head and gives up. Caused by cold stress on young transplants (more than 10 days below 50 °F before establishment). Control. Don't transplant brassicas before the soil is consistently above 60 °F. Don't expose seedlings to early frost.

sh: Physiological disorders — 5 Sunscald
b: White or tan papery patches on the sun-facing side of peppers, tomatoes, apples, melons, even tree trunks. Caused by direct intense sun on tissue not previously shaded. Control. 1. Don't strip foliage above ripening fruit; leave shade on the south and west sides. 2. 30 % shade cloth during the worst June heat. 3. Orient rows north-south for even sun exposure. 4. White latex trunk paint on young fruit trees (50/50 with water).

sh: Physiological disorders — 6 Fruit splitting / cracking
b: Sudden splitting of ripe or near-ripe tomatoes, melons, grapes, pomegranates, figs after a heavy monsoon rain. The fruit took up water faster than the skin could expand. Control. 1. Pick fruit at "color break" before a forecast heavy rain. 2. Mulch heavily to even out soil moisture. 3. Drip irrigation rather than long deep weekly soaks (the latter pattern primes splitting when rain hits). 4. Crack-resistant varieties (Juliet, Mountain Pride, Sungold among tomatoes; Suri Sahel, Wonderful among pomegranates).

sh: Physiological disorders — 7 Other physiological problems
b: Disorder | Crops | Cause | Control | Catfacing | Tomato | Cold during flower set | Plant after consistent 55 °F+ nights | Bolt-before-set | Brassicas, lettuce, cilantro | Heat stress / day length | Plant in proper season; bolt-slow varieties | Cat-eye / zippering | Tomato | Cold during set | Wait for warmer nights | Sun-scorched fruit | Peppers, tomato | Direct sun, dehydration | Leave shading foliage | Tomato leaf roll | Tomato | Heat stress, often harmless | Mulch, irrigation | Hollow heart | Potato | Inconsistent water | Even watering | Most "disease" problems in the Dragoons are actually p

sh: Small mammals
b: Packrat · gopher · ground squirrel · jackrabbit · cottontail The Dragoons have a richer small-mammal community than most American gardening regions. The four below are the ones that pressure gardens with any regularity.

sh: Small mammals — 1 Packrat (woodrat)
b: Neotoma albigula (white-throated woodrat) is the species you'll meet most often. The packrat is the iconic Cochise County mammal — a stocky brown rodent the size of a small squirrel, with a furry (not naked) tail. Builds large stick-and-debris nests (middens) in cholla, prickly pear, woodpiles, and outbuildings. Identification. Stick middens 2–4 ft across, often with bones, glass, foil, and shiny objects woven in. Chewed irrigation lines. Squash and melon fruit gnawed with tooth marks larger than a mouse but smaller than a rabbit. Tomato fruit half-eaten. Damage in the garden. Chews drip lines

sh: Small mammals — 2 Gopher
b: Pocket gopher (Thomomys bottae) — solitary, tunnel-dwelling rodent. Active year-round in the Dragoons. Lives underground, rarely seen above; identified by characteristic fan-shaped soil mounds. Damage. Eats roots of trees, perennials, root crops. Can kill a young fruit tree in a single season — symptoms are sudden wilt and decline with no above-ground cause, then the trunk wobbles when pushed because the roots are gone. Control sequence. 1. Wire baskets at planting. Quarter-inch galvanized hardware cloth shaped into a 24-inch cube around the root ball when planting any tree or perennial. Stand

sh: Small mammals — 3 Ground squirrels
b: Round-tailed ground squirrel (Spermophilus tereticaudus) and rock squirrel (Spermophilus variegatus) — both present in the Dragoons. Burrowing diurnal rodents; rock squirrels especially are bold and persistent. Damage. Eat fruit, nuts, melons, sweet corn, sometimes tomatoes. Strip seeds from sunflowers. Tunnel into garden beds. Rock squirrels can climb fruit trees. Control. 1. Trapping with snap traps or live traps; rebait daily. 2. Hardware cloth burial at fence line — 12 inches below grade prevents tunnel entry. 3. Owl boxes. Encourage great horned owl on the property; one owl can eat 1,000+

sh: Small mammals — 4 Jackrabbit and cottontail
b: Two species, different management: Black-tailed jackrabbit (Lepus californicus) — large (5–8 lb), long ears, leaps 6+ ft. Cuts through young trees and strips bark in winter. Can clear a 4-ft fence. Desert cottontail (Sylvilagus audubonii) — smaller (2–3 lb). Eats lettuce, peas, beans, brassica seedlings, low fruit. Damage. Bark stripping on young trees in winter (jackrabbit). Seedling and low-leaf grazing (both). Sharp 45-degree pruner-cuts on stems are the classic rabbit signature. Control. 1. Welded-wire fence, 5 ft minimum for jackrabbits, 4 ft for cottontails. Bury 6 inches deep or pin tig

sh: Large mammals — 1 Javelina — the Cochise large-mammal threat
b: Pecari tajacu — collared peccary. The Dragoons signature large mammal, and the single most important wildlife threat to fruits, melons, and unfenced gardens. Plant the fence before the trees. A herd that learns where the figs, pomegranates, or melons are will return every year for the rest of those plants' lives. The unfenced backyard orchard in the Dragoons is a javelina restaurant. Exclusion before they discover the food is dramatically easier than removal after. Identification. Pig-like, 30–60 lb adults, gray-black with a pale collar. Travel in herds of 6–15. Mostly nocturnal but visible at

sh: Large mammals — 2 Deer
b: White-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) at higher Dragoon elevations; mule deer (Odocoileus hemionus) at lower. Both browse hard on most garden plants. Damage. Strips foliage from fruit trees, peppers, beans, tomatoes. Stripped bark in winter. Worse in drought years when wild forage is poor. Control. 1. 8-ft fence is the only reliable exclusion. Lower fences (6 ft) work some places but mule deer can clear 6 ft. 2. Two-fence system — two 5-ft fences set 4 ft apart can work where 8-ft is prohibited; deer won't jump into the narrow space. 3. Tree tubes for individual orchard trees in fenced ga

sh: Birds
b: Netting · sparrow attacks · dove pressure · the harvest-first strategy Bird pressure in the Dragoons is real but selective. Most birds are not pests; some flocking species hit specific crops hard at specific times.

sh: Birds — 1 The species that matter
b: Curve-billed thrasher (Toxostoma curvirostre) — eats figs, peaches, apricots, tomatoes. Year-round resident. Northern mockingbird (Mimus polyglottos) — same target list as thrasher. Year-round. House finch (Haemorhous mexicanus) — strips young plantings of leaves, eats berries. Year-round. House sparrow (Passer domesticus) — attacks newly seeded beds, strips seedlings. Worst in spring. White-winged dove (Zenaida asiatica) — eats grain, sunflower, sweet corn. Summer migrant. Mourning dove (Zenaida macroura) — same as white-wing. Scrub jay, gray-breasted jay — opportunistic; eat almonds, sometim

sh: Birds — 2 Defenses
b: 1. Bird netting — 3/4 inch mesh, draped over fruiting trees or vines just before veraison (first color change). The single most effective tool. Drape, don't tent — drape allows physical contact at canopy edges, where birds learn quickly there's no food. Tent-style structures with airspace let birds get inside and trap them (mortality + crop loss). 2. Drape, then leave. Once netted, don't lift for partial harvests; lift only on harvest morning. Partial coverage teaches the birds where to enter. 3. Harvest-first strategy on figs. Visit the tree daily during ripening. Pick fruit at just-ripe befo

sh: Birds — 3 Sparrow and dove pressure on bare beds
b: Newly seeded beds — beets, carrots, lettuce — get attacked by sparrows and doves at germination. Common in spring. Control. 1. Floating row cover until plants are 3 inches tall. 2. Bird tape or scare balloons for short-term protection (effective for 1–2 weeks before habituation). 3. Pre-germination indoors. Pre-sprout seeds and transplant — bypasses the seedling-vulnerability window.

sh: Birds — 4 Sweet corn — the dove and crow problem
b: Sweet corn at silking attracts white-winged doves and Chihuahuan ravens. Control. 1. Plant in a block, not a row — denser plantings reduce per-plant access. 2. Bird tape on tassels. 3. Tight-husked varieties (Country Gentleman, Silver Queen) resist bird entry to the ear. Bird netting can kill. Snakes, lizards, and birds themselves all get tangled in poorly maintained or oversized-mesh netting. Use small-mesh netting (1/4 to 3/4 inch). Inspect netting daily. Remove and store netting immediately after harvest — don't leave it up year-round. The wildlife mortality from chronic netting is real and

sh: Reptiles as garden allies
b: Gopher snake · kingsnake · whipsnake · why to leave them alone The Dragoons are home to a remarkable reptile diversity, and most of it is on your side. The non-venomous snakes in particular are the longest-running rodent and gopher control in the garden. Learning to identify them and leaving them alone is one of the highest-return habits a Cochise County gardener can build.

sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 1 The gopher snake — your most useful reptile
b: Sonoran gopher snake (Pituophis catenifer affinis) — also called bull snake. Tan to yellow with dark brown blotches, 3–6 ft long, sometimes longer. Active diurnal and crepuscular. Why you want one in the yard. Eats pocket gophers (the name is literal), packrats, ground squirrels, mice, young rabbits. A single resident gopher snake handles more rodents in a summer than any trap, poison, or terrier ever could. Permanent residents are common in Cochise County yards with adequate shelter. How to recognize. Long, slender. Yellow-tan with squared brown blotches running the length of the back. Pointe

sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 2 Kingsnake
b: Sonoran mountain kingsnake (Lampropeltis pyromelana) and California kingsnake (Lampropeltis californiae) — both present in the Dragoons. Kingsnakes eat rodents and other snakes including rattlesnakes. The mountain kingsnake is the brilliant red-black-and-white-banded snake you'll occasionally see in the Dragoons. Why you want one. Kingsnakes are immune to rattlesnake venom and actively hunt them. A resident kingsnake reduces rattlesnake encounter risk around the house. How to recognize. California kingsnake is banded black and white (sometimes black and yellow). Sonoran mountain kingsnake has

sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 3 Other harmless snakes you might see
b: Coachwhip (Coluber flagellum) — fast diurnal, slender, pink-red or tan; eats lizards and small mammals. Western patch-nosed snake (Salvadora hexalepis) — small, fast; eats lizards. Glossy snake (Arizona elegans) — nocturnal, eats rodents. Lyresnake, night snake (Hypsiglena) — small, rear-fanged, mildly venomous but no threat to humans.

sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 4 The one snake to identify and respect
b: Western diamondback rattlesnake (Crotalus atrox) — present throughout Cochise County. Triangular head, segmented rattle, vertical pupils. Most active April–October. Will rattle as a warning if approached. The encounter protocol. 1. Stop and back away slowly. Give the snake at least 6 ft of space. 2. Don't kill it. Even rattlesnakes serve their ecological role, and killing one tends to bring others (the territory remains, the prey base remains). 3. Call a relocator. In the Sierra Vista / Tombstone / Bisbee area, several local relocators will move a snake for free; in remoter parts of Cochise Co

sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 5 Lizards as allies
b: Desert spiny lizard, side-blotched lizard, whiptail — all eat insects (grasshoppers, beetles, occasionally hornworm larvae). Encourage by leaving sunny rocks and rock-pile habitat. Greater earless lizard — fast, common, harmless. Gila monster (Heloderma suspectum) — venomous but slow and not aggressive. Protected by state law. Leave alone if seen.

sh: Dogs as garden security
b: Breed considerations · training · produce safety A dog on the property is the single most effective wildlife deterrent for some pressure types. Not all dogs and not all situations, but the right dog handles javelina, deer, jackrabbit, and packrat presence by simple deterrence.

sh: Dogs as garden security — 1 What a dog can do
b: Javelina deterrence. A dog actively patrolling — barking, occasionally chasing to the fence — keeps most javelina herds out of the immediate yard. A herd will sometimes test, but rarely settles. Deer deterrence. Less reliable; deer pressure depends heavily on local population density. A dog helps but doesn't replace fencing. Rabbit and ground squirrel deterrence. Most dogs catch or chase rabbits and squirrels; over weeks, the rabbit population learns to feed elsewhere. Packrat deterrence. Some dogs are hunters; others ignore packrats. Terriers and small hunters are best at this.

sh: Dogs as garden security — 2 What a dog can't do
b: Doesn't replace fencing for an orchard or vineyard. The dog isn't watching 24 hours; the fence is. Doesn't help with birds. Most dogs don't engage with birds reliably. Doesn't help with insect pests, obviously. Doesn't help much with snakes — and an untrained dog into a rattlesnake is a serious veterinary emergency.

sh: Dogs as garden security — 3 Breeds suited to the Dragoons
b: The right dog depends on the workload, the size of the property, and the climate (the Dragoons reach 100 °F in June and 18 °F in January). Heat-tolerant working breeds — Australian shepherd (with regular shade), Anatolian shepherd, Karelian bear dog. These are perimeter and livestock-guardian breeds. Heat-tolerant smaller hunters — Jack Russell terrier (packrat / rodent), rat terrier, fox terrier. Smaller, more nimble, good in dense plantings. Avoid heavy-coated breeds without serious shade and water. A Newfoundland is miserable in June Dragoons.

sh: Dogs as garden security — 4 Training for produce safety
b: A garden-trained dog learns to: 1. Not dig in beds. Train with "off" command and consistent correction at the first attempt. A dog that learns to dig in a tomato bed in week one will dig forever. 2. Not eat tomatoes, melons, peppers off the vine. Many dogs naturally avoid them; some develop a habit. Correct early. 3. Patrol the fence line. Walk the perimeter together early — the dog learns the boundary as its territory. 4. Rattlesnake aversion. Worth the one-day class for any Dragoons dog. Local trainers offer this annually around Sierra Vista and Tucson. Some garden plants are toxic to dogs.

sh: Fencing & exclusion
b: What works for each animal · cost · siting · sub-grade burial Fencing is the single largest infrastructure investment in pest management. A well-sited 8-ft deer fence around a half-acre orchard costs $2,500–4,000 in materials and 30–60 hours of work; the same orchard fence-free averages 50 %+ wildlife losses per year, indefinitely. The math favors fencing almost always.

sh: Fencing & exclusion — 1 Animal-by-animal exclusion requirements
b: Animal | Minimum fence | Notes | Cottontail rabbit | 3-ft welded wire, pinned to ground | 1-inch or smaller mesh at bottom | Jackrabbit | 5-ft welded wire | 1-inch mesh; bury or pin | Ground squirrel | 4-ft + 12-inch buried apron | Hardware cloth on burial portion | Pocket gopher | Hardware-cloth basket at planting | Per tree / perennial | Packrat | Hardware cloth at fence skirt | Move woodpile / cholla habitat | Javelina | 5-ft welded wire minimum; 6-ft + electric strand at 12" preferred | Bury or pin tight to ground | Deer | 8-ft fence | Or 2× 5-ft fences set 4 ft apart | Birds | Bird nettin

sh: Fencing & exclusion — 2 Material specifications
b: Welded wire (galvanized, 14 or 16 gauge) is the workhorse fencing material. 2x4-inch mesh for most uses; 1-inch mesh at the bottom 18 inches if rabbits or squirrels are in play. Roll widths typically 4 ft, 5 ft, or 6 ft. Hardware cloth (1/4 or 1/2 inch galvanized mesh) for buried portions, tree baskets, gopher exclusion. More expensive but indispensable where it matters. T-posts every 6 ft for typical garden fence; every 8 ft for heavier orchard fence with bigger gauge wire. Drive posts 18 inches into ground. Electric strand — 14-gauge poly wire, 0.5 to 2 joule charger, single strand at 12 inc

sh: Fencing & exclusion — 3 Siting considerations
b: 1. Map the runs. Animals travel on predictable paths — wash bottoms, ridge tops, fence lines, dirt road edges. Site your fence to block the run, not at random. 2. Gate placement. Put gates where they're not on the run. A gate on the deer trail is a deer trail with a gate. 3. Don't fence in a hawk corridor. Open shooting lanes for raptors are good for rodent control. A bowed-top deer fence with overhead wire is the worst case. 4. Tree-line edges. Many predators (and some pests) move along treelines. Keep at least a 4 ft mowed strip between woods and garden. 5. Drainage. Don't site fence on a wa

sh: Fencing & exclusion — 4 Sub-grade burial
b: For animals that dig under (javelina, ground squirrel, gopher, rabbit): Apron method. Bury 12 inches of hardware cloth at 90-degree angle outward from the fence base. When animal tries to dig, hits the apron and turns back. Skirt method. Pin fence wire tight to ground with landscape staples every 12 inches. Less expensive, less reliable than apron. Trench method. Bury 12–18 inches of fence vertically below grade. Most expensive but most secure.

sh: Fencing & exclusion — 5 Cost ranges (2026 dollars)
b: Fence type | Linear-foot cost (materials) | Labor (DIY hours / 100 ft) | 4-ft welded wire, T-posts | $4–6 | 4–6 | 5-ft welded wire, T-posts | $5–7 | 5–7 | 6-ft welded wire, T-posts | $7–10 | 6–8 | 8-ft deer fence, posts | $12–18 | 8–12 | Hardware cloth (3 ft tall, buried apron) | +$5–8 | +3–5 | Single electric strand | +$1–2 | +2 (plus charger $80–200) | Tree-individual basket (1/2" hardware cloth, 4 ft tall) | $25–40/tree | 1 hr/tree | For a typical 100 ft × 100 ft (400 ft perimeter) garden: Cottontail-only 3-ft fence: ~$1,600 in materials. Jackrabbit + javelina 5-ft + electric: ~$2,800 in ma

sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them
b: Parasitic wasps · lacewings · ladybugs · syrphid flies · insectary strips The beneficial insect community is the long-term solution to most pest pressure. A garden that has them needs less spraying. A garden that doesn't, never quite gets ahead. This chapter is about how to invite them in and keep them around.

sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 1 The major beneficials
b: Beneficial | Targets | What it looks like | How to attract | Lacewing (Chrysoperla) | Aphids, mites, thrips | Green delicate adults; brown alligator-shaped larvae | Cilantro, dill, fennel flowers; don't spray | Ladybug (Hippodamia, Harmonia) | Aphids, mealybugs | Red/orange round adults; black/orange alligator larvae | Pollen flowers, overwintering shelter | Syrphid fly (Toxomerus, Eupeodes) | Aphids | Hovering bee-mimic adults; gray-green larvae | Sweet alyssum, yarrow, dill | Parasitic wasp (Cotesia, Aphidius, Trichogramma) | Caterpillars, aphids, eggs | Tiny (1–5 mm) wasps; rarely seen | Sm

sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 2 Insectary strips — the canonical Dragoons mix
b: A 12 to 24-inch-wide flowering strip adjacent to the main beds is the single best beneficial-insect habitat. The Dragoons-appropriate mix: Plant | Role | Season | Cilantro / coriander | Parasitic wasp forage when in flower | Spring + fall | Dill | Parasitic wasp + syrphid forage | Spring–summer | Fennel | Long-season parasitic wasp forage | Late spring–fall | Yarrow (Achillea) | Lacewing + parasitic wasp forage | Summer | Sweet alyssum (Lobularia) | Syrphid fly forage (massive) | Cool seasons | Buckwheat | Generalist forage | Summer | Borage | Bee forage + parasitic wasp | Summer | Bachelor's

sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 3 The "never spray during bloom" rule
b: Never spray pollinator forage in bloom. Bees, native bees, and the beneficial-insect community are all most exposed when plants are flowering. Even "soft" sprays — soap, neem, spinosad — can harm them. The hard rule: if it's flowering and pollinators are visiting, don't spray. If you must intervene during bloom, hand-pick or water-blast only. This is especially important for: Brassicas in flower (bolted, or seed crops). Spring brassica bloom is critical native-bee forage in the Dragoons. Cucurbits in flower. Squash bees (Peponapis pruinosa) are specialists that emerge with the first squash blo

sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 4 Buying versus attracting
b: Most commercial beneficial-insect releases (ladybug packets, lacewing eggs, predatory mites) have mixed track records in open gardens — the insects often disperse within hours. They work in greenhouses and indoor production where the insects are confined. For outdoor gardens, the better long-term return is on habitat rather than direct release. The native beneficial-insect community is already in the Dragoons — if you stop spraying and start flowering, they show up on their own. The exceptions worth purchasing: Predatory mites for greenhouse / hydroponic systems with spider mite problems. Tric

sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 5 What kills beneficials
b: Practice / spray | Beneficials harmed | Pyrethrin | Bees, wasps, lacewings, ladybugs, syrphids — almost everything | Carbaryl (Sevin) | Same | Imidacloprid (neonicotinoids) | Bees, wasps especially | Broad-spectrum insecticidal soap | Soft-bodied beneficials (lacewing larvae, syrphid larvae) | Sulphur | Predatory mites | Diatomaceous earth | Ground beetles, some lacewing larvae | Heavy mowing | Ground habitat (ground beetles, spiders) | Bare-soil weed control | Generalist predator habitat | Most of these can be used selectively without major harm — the rule is targeted, dusk-timed, not during

sh: Spray decision tree
b: When to spray · what to spray · what NOT to spray · dusk timing This chapter is the synthesis of the rest of the guide. Most garden problems do not need a spray. When they do, the choice of spray matters. The framework below works from the gentlest, most targeted tool outward.

sh: Spray decision tree — 1 The decision sequence
b: When a pest or disease is detected, walk this sequence from the top. Stop at the first step that resolves the problem. Most garden issues never get past step 2. 1. Is the population above the action threshold? If not — wait and scout. Most damage is cosmetic and the predators are coming. 2. Is there a cultural fix? Mulch, prune, adjust watering, exclude with row cover, rotate next year. This solves more problems than any spray. 3. Mechanical fix? Hand-pick, hose-blast, sticky trap, pheromone trap. Effective for low-to-moderate populations. 4. Targeted biological? Bt for caterpillars; spinosad

sh: Spray decision tree — 2 The spray inventory — what each one does
b: Spray | Class | Target | Mode of action | Pollinator safety | Notes | Insecticidal soap | Soap | Soft-bodied insects (aphid, mite, thrip) | Disrupts cell membranes on contact | Moderate; spray dusk | Must contact insect to work | Horticultural oil | Oil | Scale, eggs, some mites | Smothers | Moderate | Don't apply >85 °F | Neem oil | Botanical | Soft-bodied + growth disruption | Disrupts molting, antifeedant | Moderate (some brands worse) | Some neem brands contain phytotoxic carriers | Bt kurstaki | Biological | Caterpillars only | Gut toxin specific to Lepidoptera | High (safe for bees) | UV

sh: Spray decision tree — 3 The hard rules
b: Three things never to do: 1. Never spray during bloom. Not pyrethrin, not neem, not even spinosad. If a plant is flowering and pollinators are visiting, don't spray it. 2. Never apply copper near waterways. Copper is acutely toxic to fish, frogs, and aquatic invertebrates. Spray dormant orchards far from streams or pond runoff. Don't rinse copper sprayer into a drain that feeds surface water. 3. Never use broad-spectrum sprays during a pest outbreak you don't understand. Pyrethrin makes spider mites worse by killing predatory mites. Carbaryl makes aphids worse by killing aphid predators. Diagn

sh: Spray decision tree — 4 Dusk timing — the safety rule
b: Most beneficial insects (especially bees) stop foraging at sunset. Most pest insects feed at dusk and night. Most sprays that aren't bee-safe become dramatically less harmful applied after sunset. The protocol: spray within 30 minutes of sunset. Allow overnight drying. By morning, most contact-acting sprays (pyrethrin, neem, spinosad) have degraded enough to be relatively safe for pollinators returning. Sprays that should NOT be applied at midday: Pyrethrin (pollinator kill) Neem (pollinator harm + leaf burn at high temperature) Spinosad (pollinator harm before drying) Sulphur (foliage burn ab

sh: Spray decision tree — 5 Neem brands — a caveat
b: Not all "neem" is the same. Cold-pressed pure neem oil with no carrier solvents is what the books mean by "neem oil." Cheaper neem products are often clarified neem (hydrophobic extract of neem oil — actually a different chemical profile, less active) or solvent-cut neem in a phytotoxic carrier. Read the label. Pure cold-pressed neem with an emulsifier (and water dilution per label) is what works without burning crops or harming pollinators excessively.

sh: Spray decision tree — 6 The "what to keep in the shed" inventory
b: For a typical Cochise County backyard garden, the practical spray inventory is small: 1. Bt kurstaki (Dipel, Thuricide) — caterpillar control. Replace yearly; loses potency. 2. Insecticidal soap (M-Pede, Safer's, homemade 1 tbsp Castile soap per quart water). 3. Neem oil (pure cold-pressed) — emergency aphid/mite/whitefly control. 4. Spinosad (Monterey Garden Insect Spray) — thrip, codling moth, leafhopper. 5. Sulphur dust or wettable sulphur — powdery mildew + spider mites. 6. Liquid copper (Bonide) — dormant peach leaf curl, fireblight. 7. Potassium bicarbonate (Kaligreen, MilStop) — powdery

sh: Annual scouting calendar
b: Month-by-month · what to watch for · when controls are needed The Dragoons pest year runs a different rhythm than most American climates — mild winters keep populations alive year-round, the dry pre-monsoon period favors mites, the monsoon brings fungal disease and cucurbit pests, the long fall extends most pest seasons by 4–6 weeks. The calendar below summarizes the 12-month rhythm for the home grower.

sh: Annual scouting calendar — 1 The year in pests
b: Month | Primary scouting targets | Active controls | January | Aphids on overwintered brassicas; packrat sign | Dormant copper spray on peach (mid-month); dormant oil on apple/pear | February | Aphids continue; codling-moth trap setup | Second dormant copper on peach; first compost amendments | March | Onion thrips on overwintered alliums; flea beetle on greens; cabbage white emerging | Row cover on spring brassicas; trap setup | April | Cabbage white peak (spring); leafhopper migration begins; spider mites on indoor/protected crops; codling moth flight 1 starts | Bt on brassicas; spinosad if

sh: Annual scouting calendar — 2 The four "must-do" dates
b: The four highest-return actions of the Dragoons pest year: 1. Mid-January and mid-February — dormant copper on peach trees. Prevents peach leaf curl. Single most important orchard action. 2. Late May — aluminum foil at base of every squash stem. Prevents squash vine borer. Single most important cucurbit action. 3. First big monsoon rain in July — pyrethrin dusk on cucumber/melon/squash within 48 hours. Controls the cucumber beetle surge that vectors bacterial wilt. 4. Late August — row cover on every fall brassica transplant. Excludes cabbage white, harlequin bug, and cutworm at the most vulne

sh: Annual scouting calendar — 3 The dormant-season checklist
b: Winter (December–February) is when most of the next year's pest pressure is decided. The dormant-season checklist: 1. Sanitation. Remove all summer-crop debris. Burn or trash anything that was infected (don't compost). Rake fruit-tree leaves and dispose. 2. Dormant copper sprays. Mid-January and mid-February on peach/nectarine. Single dormant copper on apple/pear if fireblight was an issue. 3. Dormant horticultural oil on apple, pear, and stone fruit. Smothers overwintering scale, mites, and some aphid eggs. 4. Pruning. Cut out fireblight cankers (sterilize between cuts), peach tree borer dama

sh: Annual scouting calendar — 4 The monsoon-onset trigger
b: The single most important "trigger event" in the Dragoons pest year is the first big monsoon storm — typically late June to mid-July. Within 48–72 hours of that storm: Cucumber beetle populations triple. Bacterial wilt risk peaks. Squash vine borer flight intensifies. Powdery mildew spores germinate. Hornworm moths begin egg-laying. Slug populations explode. Spider mite populations crash (humidity-killed) — moves them off the priority list. Knowing this lets you concentrate action: in the week after the first monsoon storm, plan to scout cucurbits daily, spray potassium bicarbonate preventivel

sh: Appendix A · Quick-reference table by problem
b: When you find damage and don't know what caused it, this table works backward from symptom to likely cause. Symptom | Likely cause | Section | Seedlings cut at soil level overnight | Cutworm | §6.1 | Wilted vine despite wet soil (cucurbits) | Bacterial wilt (cucumber beetle) | §9.1 | Wilted plant, gummy frass at base (squash) | Squash vine borer | §5.5 | Stippled silvery flecks on leaves | Thrips or leafhopper | §4.2, §4.4 | White powdery coating on leaves | Powdery mildew | §8.1 | Bronzed stems on tomato | Tomato russet mite | §7.2 | Holes in brassica leaves | Cabbage white / looper | §5.1 |

sh: Appendix B · Further reading and resources
b: University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Cochise County office. The canonical source for region-specific pest, disease, and crop alerts. Bulletins, master gardener program, soil and plant diagnostic services. Arizona Department of Agriculture. Pesticide regulation, licensed-applicator information, regulated-species permits (javelina, deer). Arizona Game and Fish. Wildlife relocation, conflict-resolution advice, hunting and permit information. Books worth owning: Garden Insects of North America — Whitney Cranshaw. The standard pest identification reference. The Holistic Orchard — Michael P

sh: Closing notes
b: The Dragoons garden is unusual among American gardens. The pest community is smaller than most regions, but the species are different enough that a generic gardening book gets it wrong in costly ways. The single biggest mental shift for a newcomer is moving from a "spray-when-you-see-it" reflex to a prevention-first, scout-often, intervene-precisely IPM rhythm. Five habits cover most of what matters: 1. The dusk walk. Ten minutes a day in season. No tool but a flashlight and a jar. 2. The four "must-do" dates. Dormant copper in January and February. Foil collars in May. Pyrethrin within 48 hou

sh: How to use this guide
b: Two questions sit upstream of every crop guide in this library: what is the soil under the plant doing, and where did the plant come from. This is the reference where those two questions are answered in depth. Every other guide assumes you know what is in here. The guide is in two parts: 1. Part 1 — Soil-building for the Dragoons (chapters 1–11). The Dragoons baseline, how to read soil, the amendments that move it where you want it, the four composting methods that work in dry-air country, manure sourcing, cover crops, mulching, and a five-year soil-building plan. 2. Part 2 — Seed starting & p

sh: Why soil-building is the biggest lever
b: The Dragoons baseline · what compounds over years · the OM-percentage target Most of what separates a productive Dragoons garden from a struggling one is not variety choice, not irrigation gear, not pest pressure — it is the soil. Hydroponics is the explicit workaround (Guides 2 and 3); for everything growing in the ground, soil is the system. And the Dragoons soil baseline is honest about what it asks of you: alkaline, low in organic matter, well-supplied with mineral calcium and magnesium from the same caliche that gets in your way, and shallow enough in many sites that the caliche pan itsel

sh: Why soil-building is the biggest lever — 1 The Dragoons soil baseline
b: These numbers describe a typical unworked Dragoons bed at 4,500–5,000 ft elevation in the foothills of the range. Site-to-site variation is real — a wash-bottom site can read pH 7.4 and 3% OM, an exposed ridge can read 8.3 and 0.6% — but this is the central tendency. Parameter | Dragoons typical | What target soils look like | Implication | pH | 7.6 – 8.1 | 6.0 – 7.0 (most vegetables) | Most micronutrients are partially locked out at this pH; acidification is the highest-leverage chemistry change | Electrical conductivity (EC) | 0.4 – 1.2 mS/cm | 0.3 – 0.8 mS/cm | Variable — irrigation history

sh: Why soil-building is the biggest lever — 2 Why annual investment compounds
b: A first-year Dragoons bed at 1.5% OM is producing food, but it is producing food the hard way — fast irrigation cycles, frequent fertigation, narrower margins on every crop. Each season's compost top-dress and cover-crop turn-under adds organic matter. The math is slow but real: A 2-inch annual top-dress of finished compost into a 10-inch root zone, fully incorporated, raises OM by roughly 0.5–0.8 percentage points per year — and that number drops over time because microbial respiration burns OM continuously in warm dry soil. A turned-under cover crop adds another 0.2–0.4 percentage points per

sh: Reading your soil
b: pH · EC · OM · texture (the jar test) · the UA Cooperative Extension panel Before you amend anything, know what you have. A Dragoons garden ruined by random sulfur additions is just as broken as one ruined by neglect — overshooting pH or salinity is harder to fix than starting alkaline.

sh: Reading your soil — 1 The four numbers that matter
b: These are the four readings worth knowing for any soil bed in the Dragoons. Everything else (boron, sulfate, sodium, individual micros) is useful detail; these four set the strategy. Reading | What it tells you | How to measure | Frequency | pH | Whether nutrients are chemically available to roots | Inexpensive probe ($30) or send-out lab | Annual, plus any time growth looks "stuck" | EC (electrical conductivity) | How salty the soil is (fertilizer + native salts) | Same probe (many do both); or lab | Annual, more often if you use manure | OM (organic matter, %) | How much carbon is in the soi

sh: Reading your soil — 2 pH — the master variable
b: pH (the chemistry term for acidity / alkalinity, on a 0–14 scale where 7 is neutral) determines whether the nutrients in your soil are available to plant roots. The Dragoons soil is alkaline (pH 7.6–8.1) because of three converging conditions: arid climate (rainfall does not leach calcium carbonate out of the profile), parent material rich in limestone and granite, and caliche close to the surface. At pH 7.8 and above: Iron, zinc, manganese, copper become partially insoluble — visible as interveinal chlorosis (yellow leaves with green veins) on the young growth of broccoli, peppers, blueberrie

sh: Reading your soil — 3 EC — soluble salts
